perturbation.original_text: XI. Term This Non -Disclosure Agreement shall expire on three (3) calendar years from December 31 of the year in which you execute this No n-Disclosure Agreement; provided, however, that notwithstanding the termination of this Non -Disclosure Agreement, the confidentiality obligations and covenants of this Non -Disclosure Agreement will survive indefinitely and shall not terminate, or else shal l remain in effect as long as permitted by law. GTC may revoke your access to Confidential Information at any time, with or without notice . GTC may extend the term of this Non -Disclosure Agreement in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: XI. Term This Non -Disclosure Agreement shall expire on one (1) calendar year from December 31 of the year in which you execute this No n-Disclosure Agreement; provided, however, that notwithstanding the termination of this Non -Disclosure Agreement, the confidentiality obligations and covenants of this Non -Disclosure Agreement will survive indefinitely and shall not terminate, or else shal l remain in effect as long as permitted by law. GTC may revoke your access to Confidential Information at any time, with or without notice . GTC may extend the term of this Non -Disclosure Agreement in its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the NDA term to one year, while still claiming the confidentiality obligations survive indefinitely, creates a potential issue. While Georgia law permits perpetual confidentiality, a short initial term followed by indefinite survival may be challenged as unreasonable, particularly if the information quickly loses its competitive edge.
perturbation.law_explanation: While perpetual confidentiality is possible under Georgia law, courts will still look at the reasonableness of the duration. Reducing the NDA term to one year makes the indefinitely surviving confidentiality obligations appear less reasonable, especially if the CEII data becomes outdated quickly. This could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade.

perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the NDA term to one year while stating confidentiality obligations survive indefinitely. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this creates a potential issue under Georgia law because courts assess the reasonableness of the duration of confidentiality obligations. A short initial term, coupled with indefinite survival, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly if the confidential information loses its competitive advantage quickly. Although no law snippets are directly applicable, the provided explanations strongly suggest the change creates a legal contradiction related to the reasonableness of the confidentiality period under Georgia law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information at GTC’s request or, at GTC’s option, certify destruction of the same within fifteen (15) days of a written request by GTC . Within such time period, each Recipient, if requested to do so, shall also submit to the GTC an affidavit stating that, to the best of his or her knowledge, all Confidential Information has been returned or destroyed and that Confidential Information no tes have either been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information at GTC’s request, without exception. No option will be given to certify the destruction of data.
perturbation.explanation: Removal of the option for data destruction at the discretion of GTC can potentially increase liability for the recipient, contradicting industry best practices
perturbation.contradicted_law: NIST SP 800-88 Revision 1, Guidelines for Media Sanitization
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a law, the removal of the destruction option contradicts best practices for handling sensitive information as defined by NIST. NIST SP 800-88r1 offers guidelines on media sanitization, including destruction, as a method for ensuring confidentiality. By removing the option to certify destruction, the clause forces return, potentially increasing the risk to both GTC and the Recipient of data breaches depending on the nature of the data. Although GTC owns the data, the recipient is now more liable since it could have destroyed the data but was forbidden from doing so by contract, hindering appropriate media sanitization/destruction in line with generally accepted security standards.

Information Technology Laboratory Computer Security Resource Center Publications NIST SP 800-88 Rev. 1 Guidelines for Media Sanitization Share to Facebook Share to X Share to LinkedIn Share ia Email Documentation Topics Date Published: December 2014 Supersedes: SP 800-88 (09/01/2006) Planning Note ( 03/27/2023 ): Send inquiries about this publication to [email protected] . Author(s) Richard Kissel (NIST) , Andrew Regenscheid (NIST) , Matthew Scholl (NIST) , Kevin Stine (NIST) Abstract Media sanitization refers to a process that renders access to target data on the media infeasible for a given level of effort. This guide will assist organizations and system owners in making practical sanitization decisions based on the categorization of confidentiality of their information. Media sanitization refers to a process that renders access to target data on the media infeasible for a given level of effort. This guide will assist organizations and system owners in making practical sanitization decisions based on the categorization of confidentiality of their information. Keywords media types ; mobile devices with storage ; crypto erase ; secure erase ; media sanitization ; sanitization tools and methods ; ensuring confidentiality Control Families Maintenance ; Media Protection ; Risk Assessment Documentation Publication: https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.SP.800-88r1 Download URL Supplemental Material: Sample "Certificate of Sanitization" from Appendix G (docx) Document History: 12/17/14
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the option for the recipient to certify the destruction of confidential information, mandating its return without exception. The Law Explanation indicates that this removal contradicts best practices for handling sensitive information as defined by NIST SP 800-88r1, which includes destruction as a method for ensuring confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions NIST SP 800-88r1 guidelines for media sanitization and ensuring confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that NIST SP 800-88 Rev. 1 provides guidelines for media sanitization. Therefore, by removing the option for data destruction, the modified contract introduces a legal contradiction, because it does not allow for sanitization through destruction as recommended by generally accepted security standards.

perturbation.original_text: As described above, you may exercise your right of access to Confidential Information only through individuals who: (i) have requested access to Confid ential Information from GTC and have been granted such access; (ii) need to know the information to do their jobs; (iii) have been advised of the duty of non -disclosure; and (iv) have executed this Non Disclosure Agreement by providing a signed copy of App endix A or Appendix B to GTC . You agree to keep an updated list of such individuals and to ensure that only those individuals have access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: As described above, you may exercise your right of access to Confidential Information only through individuals who: (i) have requested access to Confid ential Information from GTC and have been granted such access; (ii) have been advised of the duty of non -disclosure; and (iii) have executed this Non Disclosure Agreement by providing a signed copy of App endix A or Appendix B to GTC . You agree to keep an updated list of such individuals and to ensure that only those individuals have access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'need to know' clause for access to confidential information presents a problem.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change introduces conflict with OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard, specifically 29 CFR § 1910.1200(f)(4), which implicitly requires that access to safety data sheets (SDS) and information on hazardous chemicals be limited to those with a 'need to know' to protect worker safety. While this NDA covers CEII, the removal of the 'need to know' principle sets a precedent which can be legally troublesome if the recipient also handles hazardous materials. Allowing access without a 'need to know' increases the risk of accidental exposure or misuse of hazardous materials, violating the spirit and intent of OSHA regulations to ensure workers have appropriate and necessary information regarding workplace hazards.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Standard Number: 1910.1200 Title: Hazard Communication. Appendix: A B C D E F GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.1200(a) Purpose . 1910.1200(a)(1) The purpose of this section is to ensure that the hazards of all chemicals produced or imported are classified, and that information concerning the classified hazards is transmitted to employers and employees. The requirements of this section are intended to be consistent with the provisions of the United Nations Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS), primarily Revision 7. The transmittal of information is to be accomplished by means of comprehensive hazard communication programs, which are to include container labeling and other forms of warning, safety data sheets and employee training. 1910.1200(a)(2) This occupational safety and health standard is intended to address comprehensively the issue of classifying the potential hazards of chemicals, and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, and to preempt any legislative or regulatory enactments of a state, or political subdivision of a state, pertaining to this subject. Classifying the potential hazards of chemicals and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, may include, for example, but is not limited to, provisions for: developing and maintaining a written hazard communication program for the workplace, including lists of hazardous chemicals present; labeling of containers of chemicals in the workplace, as well as of containers of chemicals being shipped to other workplaces; preparation and distribution of safety data sheets to employees and downstream employers; and development and implementation of employee
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a key safeguard ('need to know') regarding access to confidential information, potentially conflicting with OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard. The Law Explanation argues that this creates a precedent that could lead to violations if the recipient handles hazardous materials. Scraped Law Snippet 2, under 1910.1200(a)(1), states that the purpose of the Hazard Communication Standard is to ensure that information concerning classified hazards is transmitted to employees, and that hazard communication programs include safety data sheets and employee training. While the original contract deals with Confidential Information rather than specifically hazardous materials, removing the 'need to know' clause weakens the overall control and increases potential risk related to confidential information, as well as creates a concerning precedent within the company's policies.

perturbation.original_text: F. The Recipient is required to disclose pursuant to a valid order of a court, governmental body or regulatory agency, provided that to the extent it may lawfully do so, the Recipient shall first have given written notice to GTC and given GTC a reasonable opportunity to interpose an objection or obtain a protective order , and to cooperate with GTC in taking steps to restrict or narrow the information you must disclose.
perturbation.changed_text: F. The Recipient is required to disclose pursuant to a valid order of a court, governmental body or regulatory agency, and the Recipient will not notify GTC or attempt to seek a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to notify GTC and seek a protective order contradicts legal best practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change is an issue because it directly prohibits actions (seeking a protective order) that are protected and permitted under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically Rule 45(d)(3), which outlines protections for individuals subject to subpoenas. Prohibiting the recipient from seeking a protective order is against the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly prohibits the recipient from notifying GTC or seeking a protective order, which contradicts the rights afforded to individuals subject to subpoenas under Rule 45 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. While the snippets provided don't explicitly state a right to seek protective orders, the Law Explanation asserts that this right is protected under Rule 45(d)(3). The original text provided a pathway for the recipient to seek a protective order, while the changed text forbids it, creating a direct contradiction of the recipient's rights under law.
